planting.note: Set out in warm weather where frost is absent, or keep in a large pot.

directions: Train onto a fence or arbor in a warm, sheltered spot and water regularly through the growing season. The flowers form a two-lipped, gaping structure marbled in dark maroon and greenish white, resembling an open mouth. It flowers over a long warm season and is a strong butterfly host in tropical and subtropical gardens.

planting.note: Plant in late spring in a frost-free tropical or subtropical garden.

directions: Provide a large support for this vigorous tropical climber and keep it watered during dry spells. The deeply three-lobed leaves give the plant its name, and the flowers carry a long, dangling tail below a hooded pouch. Grow it under glass in cool climates and expect blooms through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered position with good drainage.

directions: This Mediterranean species keeps its glossy leaves through mild winters and tolerates drier soils than most pipevines once established. Grow it on a wall or trellis in a warm, protected spot and water sparingly after the first year. Small brownish-purple pipe-shaped flowers appear low among the foliage from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a spot with some afternoon shade and moist soil.

directions: Site this native vine where its roots stay cool and moist, such as a streambank or a shaded fence line, and let it scramble over shrubs or a low trellis. The curved, U-shaped flowers, striped in maroon and cream, open in late winter before the leaves fully expand. It is the essential larval host for the California pipevine swallowtail, so avoid disturbing feeding caterpillars.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, fertile soil against a strong support.

directions: This hardy Asian climber grows large and needs a substantial arbor or pergola to carry its weight over time. The rounded leaves are among the biggest in the genus, and the greenish pipe-shaped flowers are said to carry a faint camphor scent. Keep the soil rich and evenly moist, and expect flowering in late spring on established plants.

planting.note: Plant in spring in free-draining soil in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: A drought-tolerant Mediterranean climber that keeps bluish-green heart-shaped leaves through mild winters. Grow it on a trellis or through a shrub in a warm border and water only during extended dry spells once established. The dark purplish-brown pipe flowers appear over a long season from winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather in a frost-free garden with rich soil.

directions: Grow this South American vine on a sturdy support in a warm, sheltered position and keep it consistently watered. The large, curved flowers are mottled in maroon and cream with a strongly cupped lower lip. It flowers through the warm months and is best kept under glass in areas that see frost.

planting.note: Set out in late spring in a warm, frost-free position.

directions: Train onto a trellis or arbor in a warm garden and provide steady moisture through the growing season. The flowers are notable for a very long, ribbon-like tail hanging below a marbled pouch. Grow it under glass in temperate regions and expect bloom across the warm months.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a hot, well-drained spot.

directions: This desert native stays low and trailing rather than climbing, threading through sparse ground cover in hot, dry sites. Give it sharp drainage and only occasional water once established. The small brownish flowers are marked to mimic a mammal's ear, and the arrow-shaped leaves feed pipevine swallowtail larvae in the arid Southwest.

planting.note: Plant in warm weather in a frost-free garden with fertile soil.

directions: A large, vigorous tropical climber that needs a big, strong structure and room to spread. Keep it watered and fed through the warm growing season for best growth. The pouched flowers are streaked greenish yellow and maroon, and the vine is a valued butterfly host in the frost-free tropics.

planting.note: Set out in warm, frost-free conditions or grow under glass.

directions: Grow this tropical climber in warmth and humidity with steady moisture and a trellis to climb. The heart-shaped leaves are attractively marked with pale silvery veins, adding foliage interest between blooms. The curved pipe flowers are patterned in maroon and cream, appearing through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, very well-drained site.

directions: This species from Chile's dry coastal scrub tolerates heat and drought and stays fairly compact, scrambling low over rocks and shrubs. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, and water only occasionally once established. The small purple-brown pipe flowers appear in the warm months among grayish, downy leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil with light shade.

directions: A vigorous Chinese climber for a large trellis, wall, or into a tree, with soft-textured leaves and downy young shoots. Keep it in cool, moist, humus-rich soil and shelter it from drying winds. Yellowish pipe-shaped flowers veined with purple appear in late spring on established plants.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown or rooted-cutting stock in spring after hard frost.

directions: This vigorous nursery selection of the common Dutchman's pipe is grown mainly from cuttings to keep its dense, screening habit uniform. Plant against a strong porch, arbor, or pergola and tie in the young stems until they twine on their own. The overlapping heart-shaped leaves make a fast privacy screen, with the small pipe flowers tucked beneath in early summer.

planting.note: Plant out once nights are warm, or keep as a greenhouse container plant.

directions: Grown for its boldly marbled calico flowers, this form is trained on a trellis in warm gardens or containers that can be sheltered from frost. Keep evenly moist through summer and pinch the tips to encourage branching. The maroon-and-white netted flowers open from summer into autumn and draw curious attention.

planting.note: Set plants out only after night temperatures stay reliably above 50F.

directions: Plant in a rich, free-draining potting mix and provide a trellis or obelisk for the twining stems to climb. Water when the top inch of soil dries and feed every few weeks through the growing season for continuous flowers. Expect the first funnel-shaped rosy blooms within a couple of months and steady flushes until the first frost.

planting.note: Move outdoors once nights are warm and settled.

directions: Grow in bright light with a small trellis, using a loose mix that drains quickly to avoid soggy roots. Let the surface dry slightly between waterings and pinch stem tips early to encourage branching. Pure white flowers with golden throats appear in loose clusters from late spring until autumn.

planting.note: A frost-tender species best given a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Provide dappled to full sun and a support for the twining stems to wind around. Keep the soil evenly moist during active growth and reduce water in the cooler months. Large rose-pink trumpets with a yellow eye open from spring into fall against glossy, deeply veined leaves.

planting.note: Set out after the last spring frost has passed.

directions: This garden hybrid is grown from cuttings; plant it against a trellis in fertile, well-drained soil. Feed lightly through summer and deadhead spent blooms to keep new buds coming. Broad pink trumpets are produced freely from early summer until frost.

planting.note: The classic large-flowered pink climber; plant out after frost.

directions: Train this vigorous cultivar up a strong trellis or post, tying in the twining shoots as they grow. Water steadily and feed every couple of weeks for a lush flush of color. Big satiny pink trumpets, five inches or more across, bloom from late spring through fall.

planting.note: A large-flowered Suntory selection; plant out when warm.

directions: Grow in full sun with a trellis and a container of quick-draining mix. Let the top of the soil dry between drinks and feed regularly for nonstop bloom. Deep crimson-red trumpets up to five inches wide open all season with little deadheading needed.

planting.note: Large pink-flowered Suntory series; set out after frost.

directions: Site in full sun and give the climbing stems something to twine around. Keep the mix lightly moist and fertilize through summer for continuous flowering. Bright pink flared trumpets appear from spring until the first frost.

planting.note: Large white blooms with a yellow throat; plant out when warm.

directions: Plant in a bright, warm spot with a support for climbing and a fast-draining potting mix. Water when the surface dries and feed every two to three weeks in the growing season. Crisp white trumpets with golden centers open steadily from late spring into fall.

planting.note: Compact, free-branching selection good for pots and baskets.

directions: Use in containers or hanging baskets where its shorter, well-branched habit shows off. Allow the top of the soil to dry between waterings and feed regularly for heavy bloom. Rich crimson flowers cover the plant from spring through frost with almost no deadheading.

planting.note: A trailing pink form well suited to baskets.

directions: Plant in full sun in a well-drained mix and let it trail or climb a small support. Water moderately and fertilize through the warm months to sustain flowering. Soft pink trumpets bloom continuously from late spring until cold weather arrives.

planting.note: Unusual warm apricot color; plant out after frost danger.

directions: Give this selection full sun and a light, quick-draining potting mix in a pot or basket. Keep it lightly moist and feed every few weeks for the best flower show. Blooms open a soft apricot-orange that deepens with age, appearing from spring through fall.

planting.note: A compact, non-climbing dipladenia for pots and edging.

directions: Plant this bushy form in a container or bed where its mounded, trailing shape can spill over the edge. Let the soil dry slightly between waterings and feed regularly in summer. Deep red flowers cover the glossy foliage from late spring until the first frost.

planting.note: Compact bushy habit; plant out once nights stay warm.

directions: Grow in full sun in a well-drained mix, allowing the top of the soil to dry between drinks. Its naturally rounded, self-branching habit needs little pinching to stay full. Bright pink flowers open continuously from spring through autumn.

planting.note: White-flowered compact dipladenia for containers.

directions: Set in a bright spot with a fast-draining mix and water when the surface feels dry. Feed every few weeks through the warm season to keep new buds forming. White trumpets with soft yellow throats appear all summer against dark, shiny leaves.

planting.note: A long-grown pink dipladenia-type selection.

directions: Provide full sun and a modest trellis for the semi-climbing stems, or let it mound in a pot. Water moderately and avoid soggy soil, which the roots dislike. Deep rose-pink flowers with pale centers bloom from spring until frost.

planting.note: A rare double-flowered pink cultivar; plant out after frost.

directions: Grow in full sun with a support for the climbing stems and a container of loose, well-drained mix. Water when the top inch dries and feed regularly for the best bloom count. Fully double, ruffled pink flowers that resemble small roses open from spring into fall.

planting.note: Sow indoors in warmth six weeks ahead of the last frost, or direct sow when the soil warms.

directions: Scarify and soak the seeds before pressing them a quarter inch into warm, moist compost. Give the vine a sturdy support and keep it fed and watered in full sun for the strongest growth. This selection trades the fiery reds for soft lemon-cream and buttery yellow spikes that bloom from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Begin seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost date for the earliest bloom.

directions: Chip and soak the seeds, then sow a quarter inch deep once the soil has warmed. Train the vigorous stems up an arch, obelisk, or tall trellis and water regularly through the heat of summer. Expect tall, arching flower spikes graduating from crimson to orange, gold, and ivory until the first frost cuts it back.

planting.note: Tuck rosettes into a north-facing crevice with lean, gritty soil.

directions: Scatter seed on a gritty surface, chill it moist for several weeks, then keep cool and bright until germination. Grow each rosette in a rocky crevice with excellent drainage; the rosette blooms once then dies, leaving offsets behind. A towering plume of white flowers appears in early summer.

planting.note: Divide and replant the mossy cushion in cool, humusy soil.

directions: Lift and split established cushions in spring or autumn and replant the pieces into moist, well-drained soil with light shade. Keep the mossy foliage from drying in summer heat, which causes brown patches. Rich rose-red flowers hover on slender stems above the green cushion in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Wedge rosette offsets into a vertical limestone crevice.

directions: Propagate by detaching side rosettes and rooting them in gritty, lime-rich mix, since this hybrid does not come true from seed. Grow the silver rosette in a crevice with perfect drainage and shelter it from summer downpours. After years of building, the main rosette throws a long arching cascade of white flowers, then dies leaving offsets.

planting.note: Root offset rosettes in gritty soil and set into a wall or trough.

directions: Increase by rooting the small offset rosettes, as the named clone is grown from division rather than seed. Plant in sharply drained, limy soil where it gets bright light but shade from harsh afternoon sun. Arching sprays carry many white flowers heavily flecked and blotched with red in late spring.

planting.note: Grow the lime-crusted cushion in a trough of sharp alpine grit.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty limestone mix and give it a cold moist period before cool bright conditions to sprout. Keep the silver-edged cushion in very free-draining soil and shelter it from persistent winter wet. Clusters of white to pale pink cups open on short stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant the small cushion in gritty scree or a trough.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty soil and chill briefly, then keep cool and bright for prompt germination. Grow the bristly evergreen cushion in very sharp drainage and full sun, keeping it dry in winter. Bright yellow four-petaled flowers stud short stems in early spring.

planting.note: Grow the tiny hard cushion in a trough or scree bed.

directions: Sow the fine seed on gritty mix, cold-treat it briefly, and germinate cool and bright. This species makes a dense mossy dome, so give it perfect drainage and dry winter conditions. Tiny yellow flowers on short stems cover the cushion in early to mid spring.

planting.note: Set the mossy cushion into a gritty raised bed or trough.

directions: Surface-sow on grit and give a short cold period, then grow on cool and bright. The needle-leaved cushion needs full sun, lean soil, and freedom from winter damp to stay tight. Golden-yellow flowers rise just above the green mound in spring.

planting.note: Grow the white-flowered cushion in sharp scree.

directions: Sow on gritty soil, stratify cold, and germinate in cool bright conditions. This is one of the few white-flowered whitlow-grasses, forming a dense cushion that resents winter wet. Small clusters of white flowers open on short stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant the compact cushion in a sunny gravelly bed.

directions: Surface-sow on grit with a short chilling period and grow on cool and bright. Give the tight cushion baking sun, very sharp drainage, and dry winters. Bright yellow flowers appear on wiry stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant runnered rosettes in gritty humus with good drainage.

directions: Sow fresh seed and give it a cold moist spell, as germination can be slow and uneven. This species spreads by strawberry-like runners rooting new rosettes, so pin them into gritty, humus-rich soil. Rounded heads of pink flowers with a yellow eye open on short stems in late spring.

planting.note: Grow the small tuft in gritty scree or a trough.

directions: Surface-sow and stratify cold and moist over winter for best germination. Grow the little grassy tuft in sharply drained gritty soil with cool roots and shelter from winter wet. Small heads of pink or white flowers with a yellow throat open on short stems in spring.

planting.note: Let the silvery trailing stems drape over rocks or a wall.

directions: Sow fresh seed after a cold moist period, or root soft cuttings of the trailing stems. Plant on a gritty slope or wall top where the silvery, silky-haired stems can trail and drain freely. Soft pink flower heads appear over a long summer season.

planting.note: Grow the mat of tight rosettes in a trough with sharp grit.

directions: Surface-sow with a cold moist stratification, or detach and root the runner-borne rosettes. This species makes neat houseleek-like rosettes spreading by short stolons in gritty, well-drained soil. Clustered pink flowers open just above the mat in spring.

planting.note: Grow the silvery cushion in a trough or sharp scree.

directions: Sow fresh seed and give a long cold moist spell, expecting slow germination. Grow the silky white-haired cushion in very free-draining grit and keep it dry over winter. White flowers with a pink or yellow eye nestle in the cushion in late spring.

planting.note: Plant the mat in humus-rich, gritty soil that stays cool and moist.

directions: Sow fresh seed and stratify it cold and moist for several weeks, as germination is slow and erratic. Grow the evergreen mat in deep, gritty, humus-rich soil kept cool and evenly moist for reliable flowering. Enormous deep blue trumpets open almost stemless above the foliage in spring.

planting.note: Set small tufts into gritty, humus-rich soil in a trough.

directions: Surface-sow fresh seed and give a long cold moist stratification, since old seed germinates poorly. Grow the little rosettes in gritty, humus-rich soil that never dries out, ideally in a trough for close viewing. Brilliant sky-blue star-flowers open on short stems in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in cool, moist, acid, peaty soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Sow fresh seed after cold moist stratification, or lift and divide the fibrous crowns in spring. This gentian demands lime-free, cool, moist, humus-rich soil and resents drying out at any time. Large trumpet flowers of deep blue with greenish stripes open in autumn.

planting.note: Plant clumps in gritty, humus-rich soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Sow seed after a cold moist period; this is one of the easier gentians to raise from seed. Grow the arching leafy stems in fertile, well-drained soil that stays reasonably moist through summer. Clusters of deep blue trumpets with paler crested throats open at the stem tips in late summer.

planting.note: Grow the mat in gritty, limy soil kept cool and moist.

directions: Sow fresh seed and stratify it cold and moist, expecting slow and uneven germination. Unlike the acid-loving trumpet gentian, this species prefers gritty, lime-rich soil kept cool at the root. Large deep blue trumpets without inner spotting open close to the foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant in gritty, limy, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Sow fresh seed with a long cold moist stratification and grow on cool and bright. This lime-tolerant gentian forms a spreading mat of narrow leaves and takes to gritty alkaline soil. Large sky-blue trumpets open on short stems in late spring, sometimes with a second flush.

planting.note: Divide crowns in spring and replant into cool, acid, peaty soil.

directions: Propagate this hybrid by dividing the fibrous crowns in spring rather than from seed. Grow it in lime-free, cool, moist, humus-rich soil and never let it dry out at the root. Large azure-blue trumpets with striped throats open freely in autumn.

planting.note: Grow in gritty, humus-rich soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Sow seed after a cold moist period; this species germinates fairly readily for a gentian. Grow the fine-leaved arching stems in well-drained, humus-rich soil kept moderately moist. Deep blue trumpets with white-striped throats open at the stem tips in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in gritty, limy, well-drained soil kept cool.

directions: Sow fresh seed with a long cold moist stratification and grow cool and bright. This trumpet-type gentian tolerates lime and wants gritty, well-drained soil that stays cool at the root. Large deep blue trumpets open almost stemless above the rosettes in late spring.

planting.note: Give this deep-rooted giant a permanent spot in rich, moist soil.

directions: Sow fresh seed and give a prolonged cold moist stratification, as germination is very slow. This large deep-rooted gentian dislikes being moved, so site it permanently in deep, rich, moist soil. Whorls of starry yellow flowers climb tall stems in summer after several years of growth.

planting.note: Plant in cool, moist, humus-rich soil in light shade.

directions: Sow fresh seed after a cold moist period, or divide established clumps carefully in spring. This taller gentian thrives in cool, moist, humus-rich woodland-edge soil with light shade. Arching willowy stems carry deep blue trumpets in the leaf axils in late summer.

planting.note: Divide the mossy cushion and replant in cool, humusy soil.

directions: Split established cushions in spring or autumn and replant the divisions into moist, well-drained soil in light shade. Keep the mossy green foliage from scorching in summer sun and drought. Compact stems carry rich pink flowers just above the cushion in mid spring.

planting.note: Grow the silver-crusted cushion in a limestone crevice or trough.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty limy mix after cold stratification and grow on cool and bright. This species builds tight domes of silver lime-encrusted rosettes and needs very sharp drainage. Airy sprays of white flowers on red stems rise in early summer.

planting.note: Grow the stiff-leaved cushion in a sunny scree or trough.

directions: Sow on grit with a short cold treatment and germinate cool and bright. This whitlow-grass makes a firm cushion of bristly leaves that needs full sun and very sharp drainage. Yellow flowers stand on short stems above the cushion in early spring.

planting.note: Pin runnered rosettes into gritty, humus-rich soil.

directions: Sow fresh seed after cold moist stratification, or root the runner-borne rosettes into gritty mix. This spreading rock jasmine roots as it runs, so give it humus-rich, sharply drained soil. Heads of pink flowers with a yellow eye that ages red open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in cool, moist, acid, humus-rich soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Sow fresh seed after cold moist stratification, or divide the crowns carefully in spring. Like other Asian autumn gentians it wants lime-free, cool, moist, humus-rich soil that never dries. Turquoise-blue trumpets with a white throat and green stripes open in autumn.

planting.note: Grow the silvery rosette in a trough with limy grit and winter cover.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty limestone mix after cold stratification and germinate cool and bright. Grow the silver rosette in sharp drainage and shelter it from winter wet under glass or an overhang. A curved crimson-stemmed spike lined with red-purple bracts and small flowers rises in early spring.

planting.note: Grow the soft green dome in an alpine house or covered trough.

directions: Sow on grit with a short cold spell and grow on cool and bright. This species forms a soft, downy emerald dome that hates overhead wet, so it is often grown under cover. A haze of yellow flowers on fine stems covers the cushion in spring.

planting.note: Plant in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Sow seed after a cold moist period, or divide clumps in spring. This gentian likes cool, moist, humus-rich, lime-free soil and light shade in warmer areas. Upright and arching stems carry deep blue bell-shaped flowers in autumn.

planting.note: Grow the small hairy mat in gritty scree kept cool at the root.

directions: Sow fresh seed and give a long cold moist stratification, expecting slow germination. Grow the low hairy mat in gritty, well-drained soil with cool roots and protection from winter wet. Fragrant white flowers with a yellow eye that flushes pink open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant into gritty, fast-draining soil in early spring.

directions: Give this tight alpine cushion very sharp drainage and full sun, ideally in a scree bed or rock crevice. Keep it lean and avoid rich feeding, which loosens the habit. Small pink to lavender flowers stud the compact mound in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, moist woodland soil.

directions: Set into humusy soil in part shade and mulch lightly with leaf mold to hold moisture. Let the rooting stems spread to form a groundcover among stones. Fragrant deep violet-purple flowers appear on short stems in spring.

planting.note: Start seed indoors eight weeks before the last frost and surface-sow for light.

directions: Surface-sow indoors under bright light and transplant seedlings into lean, well-drained ground after frost. Cut the mat back by half after flowering to keep it dense. Deep blue-violet flowers cover the foliage in spring.

planting.note: Start indoors before the last frost; press seed onto the surface.

directions: Grow on in bright light and move plants to a sunny wall or slope with sharp drainage. Shear after bloom to renew the trailing stems. Rosy-red flowers spill down the mat in early to mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or potted plants in spring into gritty, limey soil.

directions: This old favorite comes true only from cuttings or division, so plant potted stock in full sun. Trim hard after flowering to keep the cushion tight. Rich violet-purple flowers smother the mat in spring.

planting.note: Start seed indoors before the last frost, surface-sowing for light.

directions: Sow on the surface indoors and set plants out in lean, sharply drained soil after frost. Shear the mat after flowering to prevent open centers. Deep purple flowers cover the foliage through spring.

planting.note: Start seed indoors before the last frost and surface-sow for light.

directions: Grow seedlings on in bright light and set out in lean, well-drained soil in full sun. Cut back after bloom to maintain a tidy cushion. Clean white flowers blanket the gray foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring; the double form does not come true from seed.

directions: Because the double flowers set little seed, plant cuttings or divisions in gritty, sunny ground. Shear spent stems to keep the mat neat. Fully double white flowers resembling tiny stocks cover the plant in spring.

planting.note: Start indoors before the last frost; surface-sow and keep bright.

directions: Sow on the surface indoors and transplant into gritty, fast-draining soil after frost. This West Coast native forms neat rosettes and dislikes soggy winters. Fragrant rose-pink flowers appear over the foliage in spring.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring; seed needs light and germinates quickly.

directions: Sow on the surface of lean, gravelly soil in full sun and avoid rich, damp ground that causes rot. Cut the mat back by a third after flowering to keep it from splitting open. Dense clouds of golden-yellow flowers smother the gray foliage in mid-spring.

planting.note: Start indoors a few weeks before the last frost, surface-sowing for light.

directions: Grow this tighter, denser selection in full sun with excellent drainage. Shear lightly after bloom to hold the compact form. Bright yellow flowers cover the mound in mid-spring.

planting.note: Start indoors before the last frost and surface-sow for light.

directions: Set plants in lean, sharply drained soil in full sun and keep feeding minimal. Trim after flowering to prevent the mat from flopping open. Soft lemon-yellow flowers cover the gray foliage in spring, a gentler tone than the species.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or potted plants in spring in gritty, sunny ground.

directions: This unusual apricot-toned form is best grown from cuttings to hold its color. Plant in full sun with very sharp drainage and shear after bloom. Soft buff-apricot flowers cover the mat in mid-spring.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring into gritty, alkaline soil in full sun.

directions: Press seed onto lean, stony soil and give it full sun and sharp drainage. This low silvery mat is drought-tough once established and resents wet ground. Fragrant honey-scented yellow flowers cover the plant in late spring.

planting.note: Direct-sow near the last frost, pressing seed onto the surface.

directions: Surface-sow this low, spreading white selection and keep evenly moist to germinate. Trim after the first flush to keep bloom coming through the season. Fragrant white flowers form a dense carpet from late spring to frost.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring into gritty, alkaline soil in full sun.

directions: Sow into lean, limey, fast-draining soil and give the plant full sun and lean conditions. The blue-green foliage forms a low subshrub that dislikes winter wet. Loose sprays of soft pink flowers rise above the foliage in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Best in a trough or covered alpine bed; plant offsets in spring.

directions: Plant the fuzzy, gray-green rosettes edged in fine hairs into very gritty soil, ideally under an overhang that keeps winter rain off. Overhead moisture rots the hairy crowns quickly, so bottom-water or avoid wetting the leaves. Greenish-yellow flowers appear on short curved stalks in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring into fast-draining soil.

directions: Set this vigorous green rosette with red-brown leaf tips into gritty soil in full sun. It is a reliable, easy grower that quickly forms broad mats. Water sparingly and let the chicks spread to cover a slope or wall.

planting.note: Plant webbed offsets in spring; keep crowns dry.

directions: Plant this densely cobwebbed cultivar into very gritty soil where the white threads over each rosette stay dry. It forms tight little balls of webbed green flushed with red. Avoid overhead water in winter and multiply it by detaching the small chicks.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring or early summer.

directions: Grow this two-tone cultivar with red centers grading to green-tipped outer leaves in full sun and lean soil. The bold color contrast peaks in spring and fall. Keep it dry and let the offsets spread into a broad mat.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring into fast-draining soil.

directions: Set these glossy red rosettes with contrasting green tips into gritty soil in full sun. It is a large, showy grower that keeps strong color through much of the year. Water sparingly and detach the chicks to increase the planting.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring; a rare yellow-toned form.

directions: Grow this unusual chartreuse-to-yellow houseleek in bright but not scorching sun to protect the pale leaves. Lean, gritty soil keeps the color clean and the rosettes tight. Water minimally and expect a warmer gold flush in cool weather.

planting.note: Divide and replant offsets in spring or summer.

directions: Grow this large, vigorous rosette in shades of red and green in full sun and lean soil. It is an easy, robust cultivar that forms broad clumps quickly. Water sparingly and let the abundant chicks fill in the surrounding space.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring or early summer.

directions: Plant this large rosette in warm coppery reds and greens into gritty soil in full sun. It makes a substantial single rosette before chicks encircle it. Water sparingly and let the offsets spread across a rock garden or wall.

planting.note: Increase by splitting the rosette itself, as it does not make loose chicks.

directions: Plant these firm rosettes, often edged in fine cilia and colored bronze to purple, into very gritty soil in full sun. Unlike true houseleeks, this species does not send out offsets on stolons; instead the crown divides internally, so propagate by cutting the clustered rosette apart. Pale yellow flowers open on a short stalk in summer.

planting.note: Gather the loose rolling offsets and press them into soil to root.

directions: Grow these light-green rosettes in gritty soil and full sun where their loosely attached offset balls can detach and roll away to root nearby. This tumbling habit gives the plant its common name and spreads it naturally. Greenish-yellow flowers appear on upright stalks in summer.

planting.note: Root the small rolling offsets in spring or summer.

directions: Plant these tight green rosettes, often edged red and fringed with fine hairs, into sharp soil in full sun. Like other rollers, it produces detachable offset balls that scatter to form new plants. Keep it lean and dry and expect yellow summer flowers on short stems.

planting.note: Root detached offset balls in spring into gritty soil.

directions: Set these smooth, bright-green globular rosettes into fast-draining soil in full sun. It forms very tight little balls and multiplies by shedding offsets that roll off to root. Keep winter moisture off the crowns and let it colonize a trough or scree bed.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; keep crowns dry through winter.

directions: Plant these flat, gray-green hairy rosettes into very gritty soil in full sun, ideally where rain is shielded in winter. This houseleek relative resents wet crowns and rots easily in soggy conditions. Small pale bell-shaped flowers open on short stalks in summer.

planting.note: Divide the small cushions in spring into scree soil.

directions: Grow this tiny high-alpine cushion of small green rosettes in sharp scree and full sun. It is a true miniature best suited to troughs and rock crevices where drainage is perfect. Keep it dry over winter and enjoy the small whitish-pink summer flowers.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring into gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Set these gray-green, softly hairy rosettes into sharp soil in full sun with protection from winter wet. The felted leaves hold water and rot if the crowns stay damp. Creamy to pale-yellow flowers open on short stalks in early summer.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring into fast-draining alpine soil.

directions: Plant these firm green rosettes into gritty soil in full sun, sheltering them from winter rain. Good drainage is essential to prevent rot in this fleshy alpine. Pale flowers rise on short stalks in summer above the clustered rosettes.

planting.note: Plant the runner-borne offsets in spring or summer.

directions: Grow these small gray-green rosettes in gritty, fast-draining soil in full sun to light shade. It spreads by thread-like runners into a low colony and goes semi-dormant into tight buds in winter. In late summer a mature rosette elongates into a tapered cone of tiny flowers, then dies as offsets carry on.

planting.note: Root the runner offsets in spring into gritty soil.

directions: Plant these ghostly gray-lavender rosettes into sharp soil in full sun to part shade where drainage is perfect. Runners extend to form new rosettes, building a spreading mat over a season. In autumn a rosette pushes up a tall pagoda-like spike of pale flowers and then dies, replaced by its offsets.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; extremely cold-hardy.

directions: Set these tight green rosettes, each leaf ending in a small spine, into very gritty soil in full sun. This is one of the hardiest of its group and forms firm winter buds through hard frost. A mature rosette sends up a dense cone of greenish-yellow flowers in late summer, then dies as offsets replace it.

planting.note: Root offsets in spring into fast-draining soil.

directions: Grow these green to reddish rosettes in gritty soil and full sun with excellent drainage. It forms low colonies and contracts into tight buds during winter dormancy. A flowering rosette elongates into a spike of small white flowers in autumn and then dies back to its offsets.

planting.note: Plant runner offsets in spring into gritty soil.

directions: Plant these small blue-gray rosettes into sharp, fast-draining soil in full sun to light shade. Slender runners carry new rosettes outward to build a spreading colony. In late season a mature rosette forms a tapering spike of pale flowers and then dies, leaving its offsets to continue.

planting.note: Unlike common bugleweed this species lacks runners, so it stays where planted.

directions: Plant the hairy-leaved clumps at soil level and space them evenly since they spread only by slow crown expansion. This non-running species is a good choice where bugleweed's wandering habit is unwelcome. Upright blue flower spikes rise in late spring.

planting.note: A slow, non-spreading clumper best used in small drifts or rock gardens.

directions: Plant the crinkled, metallic-bronze rosettes level with the ground in gritty, well-drained soil. Growth is slow and tight, so it stays as a distinct crimped cushion rather than a running mat. Squat blue spikes appear low among the puckered leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring or fall on slopes and shaded banks.

directions: Set plants so the trailing stems lie against the soil and pin any long shoots to encourage rooting at the nodes. Once established it needs little care and holds soil well on shaded slopes. Blue-violet pinwheel flowers open in early to mid spring with scattered rebloom.

planting.note: A clumpier, heavier-flowering form; plant in drifts under trees.

directions: Plant rooted divisions at soil level in humus-rich, shaded ground. This selection mounds a little more and carries larger, deeper blue flowers than the plain species. Bloom peaks in mid spring with a light second flush later in the season.

planting.note: Grown mainly for gold foliage; give bright filtered light to keep leaves yellow.

directions: Plant in spring where the golden-centered leaves get morning sun but shelter from harsh afternoon heat. The bright foliage lights up shady corners and cascades over walls and containers. Blue flowers appear in spring above the yellow-and-green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in shade or part shade for the richest wine-purple flower color.

directions: Set rooted stems flat against loosened soil and keep moist while roots take hold. The dark reddish-purple flowers stand out against glossy green leaves in shaded plantings. Main bloom comes in early to mid spring.

planting.note: White flowers brighten deep shade where blue forms can look dull.

directions: Plant in humus-rich shaded soil and pin trailing stems to speed coverage. The pure white pinwheel flowers read especially well in low light beneath trees. Flowering peaks in spring with scattered blooms afterward.

planting.note: A white-edged sport of the blue-flowered form; plant in bright shade.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings level with the ground and water in well. The green leaves carry a thin creamy-white margin that lightens shaded beds even out of bloom. Blue flowers cover the mat in spring.

planting.note: A compact, small-leaved white form for edging and smaller spaces.

directions: Plant the fine-textured, small-leaved stems in shaded, well-drained soil. This selection is tidier and less rampant than the species, suiting borders and containers. Abundant small white flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Widely used as a spiller in mixed containers as well as a groundcover.

directions: Plant in part shade in spring and trim wayward stems to keep it in bounds. The cream-margined leaves brighten shade and cascade attractively over container rims and walls. Blue flowers dot the trailing stems in spring.

planting.note: A classic dry-shade groundcover; plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring.

directions: Space the rooted stems a few inches apart in rich, acidic, shaded soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Plants spread by underground runners to form an even evergreen carpet under trees. Small white flower spikes appear in spring above the whorled leaves.

planting.note: Selected for unusually glossy leaves; plant in shade like the species.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in humus-rich shaded soil and keep moist while they knit together. The exceptionally lustrous dark leaves give this form extra shine in low light. Modest white spikes open in spring.

planting.note: Slower and less vigorous than the green form; site in reliable shade.

directions: Plant in cool, shaded, well-drained soil and protect from strong sun that can scorch the pale margins. The white-edged leaves lighten dim corners but the plant spreads more slowly than the plain species. Small white spring flowers top the stems.

planting.note: An extremely low, dense form ideal between stepping stones.

directions: Plant the tiny divisions close together in moist, well-drained soil where they hug the ground tightly. This ground-hugging form tolerates light foot traffic and fills paving crevices with a fine green mat. Small yellow star-shaped flowers dot the mat in summer.

planting.note: A very low creeper that tolerates light foot traffic between pavers.

directions: Plant the low rooted stems on moist, well-drained soil where they root at the nodes and hug the ground. It fills gaps between stepping stones and takes occasional footsteps once established. Purple-blue flowers with white-and-yellow throats open in late spring.

planting.note: The white-flowered form of creeping mazus for paths and crevices.

directions: Plant divisions on damp, well-drained soil and firm them so the creeping stems root quickly. This form makes the same low, traffic-tolerant carpet as the species but with clean white blooms. White flowers appear in late spring above the tight green mat.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Set plugs into lean, sharply drained soil at the depth they grew in the pot and space them to knit together in a season. Shear lightly after flowering to keep the mat dense and tidy. Deep magenta-red blooms cover the foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Set small plugs in spring, spacing tightly for this slow spreader.

directions: Plant this miniature form into lean, well-drained soil and keep it out of soggy sites. It forms an exceptionally tight, low cushion ideal for tucking between flagstones. Tiny lavender-pink flowers dot the mat in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Set plants into gritty, well-drained soil and water only until roots take hold. The gray-green foliage forms a durable low carpet that shrugs off drought. Soft salmon-pink flowers smother the mat in early summer and attract pollinators.

planting.note: Transplant out after the last frost into hot, dry, well-drained ground.

directions: Surface-sow seed and press it in without covering, or set plugs into gritty, sharply drained soil. Withhold heavy watering, as wet winter soil is the main cause of loss. Vivid magenta daisy-like flowers open in sun from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or plugs in spring into dry, gritty soil.

directions: Set plants into lean, fast-draining soil and keep water to a minimum, especially in winter. This is among the hardiest ice plants and forms a fleshy evergreen carpet that reddens in cold. Bright yellow flowers cover it in late spring.

planting.note: Plant potted starts in spring into sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant into gritty soil on a slope or raised bed where drainage is excellent. It needs full sun and dry feet to thrive and overwinter reliably. Tricolor flowers of orange, magenta, and a white eye erupt in spring.

planting.note: Plant plugs in spring into dry, well-drained ground.

directions: Set into gritty, fast-draining soil in a hot, sunny spot and avoid soggy conditions. It stays compact and forms a tight succulent cushion. Golden-yellow flowers smother the foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Start seed indoors or plant plugs after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep barely moist, or set plugs into lean, well-drained soil. It thrives in poor dry ground and forms a silvery-gray mat. Fuzzy pink or white flower clusters resembling a cat's paw rise in late spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors or plant divisions in spring.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface of gritty soil and keep just damp until it sprouts. This native tolerates poor dry ground and spreads slowly into a groundcover patch. Whitish flower heads on short stems appear in spring and support small butterflies.

planting.note: Plant in late spring into moist ground once warm.

directions: Plant into moist to seasonally wet soil, as this native favors damp meadows and pond edges. It spreads by rooting stems to form a low mat that anchors soil. Small white-and-pink flower heads bloom through the warm season for pollinators.

planting.note: Plant plugs in spring into moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Set plugs into evenly moist, well-drained soil and space them to fill in over a season. It forms a dense low mat that handles light foot traffic between pavers, preferring afternoon shade in hot regions. Tiny pale-blue star-shaped flowers stud the foliage from spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring.

directions: Set plants into moist, well-drained soil in part shade to full sun and water to establish. It spreads quickly by runners into a weed-smothering carpet, so site it where spread is welcome. Spikes of blue-violet flowers rise above the rosettes in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring once nights stay reliably above freezing.

directions: Nestle the rosette into fast-draining gritty mix so the base sits just at the surface and no leaves are buried. Water only when the soil has dried completely, easing off in winter to prevent rot. Arching pink stalks tipped with yellow-and-pink bells appear in spring on established plants.

planting.note: Plant offsets or cuttings in spring after the last frost.

directions: Grow in sharply drained soil and give strong light to bring out the red leaf edges that give this plant its name. Let the mix dry between waterings and keep water off the tight rosette. Slender pink-and-orange flower stalks rise in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Root leaf or stem cuttings in spring and transplant once established.

directions: Plant this pastel pink-and-lavender hybrid in gritty mix and give it bright light to hold its color. Allow the soil to dry fully between drinks and avoid wetting the powdery leaf coating. Coral bells on tall arching stems appear from spring into summer.

planting.note: Take stem cuttings in spring and let them callus a few days before planting.

directions: Set rooted cuttings in a coarse, quick-draining blend and place in strong sun to deepen the red leaf tips. The fuzzy leaves resent overhead water, so irrigate at the base and only when dry. Bright orange-yellow flowers open on short stalks in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Divide offsets and replant in spring as growth resumes.

directions: Plant the hair-covered rosette in gritty soil and water carefully from below to keep the fine white bristles clean and dry. Give it bright light and let the mix dry out between waterings. Nodding red-and-yellow flowers rise on wiry stems in spring.

planting.note: Root leaf or offset cuttings in spring and pot on when established.

directions: Grow in fast-draining mix and give ample sun to darken the leaves to their signature near-black. Water only when the soil is bone dry and keep moisture out of the crown. Deep red flowers on dark stalks appear in autumn and early winter.

planting.note: Separate offsets and replant in spring for best establishment.

directions: Plant this silvery rosette with its curiously upturned, tube-like leaves in a gritty, well-drained blend. Let it dry fully between waterings and give bright light to keep the form tight. Orange-yellow flowers arch out on curved stems in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Replant clustering offsets in spring.

directions: Set the compact blue-green rosettes into sharply drained soil where they will quickly form clumps of offsets. Water sparingly and keep the crown dry to avoid rot. Short stalks of orange-tipped yellow bells appear from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring as the weather warms.

directions: Grow the silvery-lilac rosette in gritty mix with strong light and minimal water, letting it dry out fully between drinks. Handle gently, as the fine powdery coating rubs off easily. Coral-pink bells on arching red stems open in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Divide and replant offsets in spring.

directions: Plant this frilly-edged, powder-blue rosette in fast-draining soil and give it bright light to enhance the pink margins. Water only when dry and keep the crown clear of standing moisture. Pink flowers rise on tall arching stalks in summer.

planting.note: Root leaf cuttings in spring, then transplant.

directions: Set this symmetrical pearly-lavender hybrid into gritty, well-drained mix and grow in bright light for the cleanest color. Let the soil dry thoroughly between waterings and avoid wetting the rosette. Coral-pink bells appear on slender stalks in spring.

planting.note: Root leaf or stem cuttings in spring and pot on.

directions: Give this large powdery-pink hybrid a coarse, fast-draining medium and full sun to bring out its rosy lavender glow. Water at the base only when the soil is dry and protect it from frost. Deep orange-red flowers rise on tall stalks in summer.

planting.note: Replant the freely produced offsets in spring.

directions: Plant these blue-gray rosettes in well-drained soil where they will spread into tight colonies of offsets. Water sparingly and keep the crowns dry to prevent rot. Orange-red bells on arching stems appear in spring.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Grow this chalky silver-blue rosette in gritty mix and bright light to hold its powdery finish and pink edges. Water only when dry and never leave water sitting in the crown. Coral-red bells rise on reddish stalks in spring and summer.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring and replant.

directions: Plant the blue-green rosette, edged in red under good light, into sharply drained soil. Allow it to dry fully between waterings and keep the crown dry. Yellow bell-shaped flowers arch out on red stems in spring.

planting.note: Root leaf cuttings in spring and pot on once rooted.

directions: Set this large silvery rosette with red-tipped leaves into gritty, fast-draining mix under strong light. Water only when the soil dries out and keep moisture away from the leaves. Pink-and-orange bells rise on tall stalks in spring.

planting.note: Take stem cuttings in spring and let them callus before planting.

directions: Grow this branching, velvety-leaved shrublet in coarse mix with bright light to redden the leaf tips. Water at the base and only when the soil is dry. Unusually large red-and-yellow lantern flowers open in spring.

planting.note: Replant the numerous offsets in spring.

directions: Plant the blue-green red-edged rosettes in well-drained soil where they spread readily into clumps. Keep water off the crowns and let the soil dry between waterings. Red-and-yellow bells rise on arching stalks in late spring and summer.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring and transplant when established.

directions: Set this upright grower in fast-draining mix and give strong light to intensify the red pencil-line markings on each pale leaf. Water only when dry and keep the crown clear of moisture. Red-and-yellow flowers open on tall stalks in spring.

planting.note: Root cuttings or offsets in spring.

directions: Plant this large pale green rosette with pink-edged leaves into coarse, quick-draining soil under bright light. Water sparingly, letting the mix dry fully between drinks. Coral-pink bells rise on tall arching stems in winter and spring.

planting.note: Root leaf cuttings in spring and pot on.

directions: Give this ruffled purple-pink hybrid gritty soil and the brightest light you can to electrify its neon margins. Water only when dry and keep the frilly crown free of standing water. Pink-and-orange bells open on tall stalks in summer.

planting.note: Root stem or leaf cuttings in spring.

directions: Plant this fuzzy silver-green rosette in coarse mix and water carefully at the base to keep the fine hairs dry. Strong light deepens the red leaf tips and keeps growth compact. Orange-yellow bells rise on short stalks in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Separate the abundant offsets and replant in spring.

directions: Set this fast-clumping green rosette into well-drained soil where cool nights and bright light turn the leaf tips orange-red. Let the soil dry between waterings and keep the crown dry. Yellow bells appear on arching stalks in spring.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring and transplant once rooted.

directions: Grow this large, tall-stemmed species in coarse, fast-draining mix with full sun. Water when the soil dries and stake older plants that develop a leggy trunk. Tall branching stalks of red flowers rise in autumn.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or dropped leaves in spring.

directions: Plant this trailing, ghostly gray-pink succulent in gritty mix where it will sprawl and root as it goes. It is remarkably drought-tough, so water only when the soil is fully dry. Sun and cool nights shift the color toward pink and yellow, and small white star flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring and pot on.

directions: Set this chunky, pebble-leaved succulent into fast-draining soil with strong light to bring out its amethyst and pink tones. Water only when the soil dries fully and keep the fat leaves from sitting in moisture. Red-and-yellow star flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Replant offsets in spring as growth resumes.

directions: Grow this flat, tightly triangular gray rosette in gritty mix with bright but not scorching light. Water sparingly and keep the low crown dry. It is prized for showy sprays of bright pink star flowers held above the plant in spring.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring and transplant.

directions: Plant this lavender-pink rosette in coarse, fast-draining soil and give it full sun to hold its pastel color. Let the mix dry completely between waterings and avoid wetting the powdery leaves. Yellow-and-red star flowers open in spring.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring and pot on when rooted.

directions: Set this flat rosette of powdery lilac-gray leaves into gritty mix under strong light for the best purple cast. Water only when dry and keep moisture off the crown. Small yellow star flowers appear on branched stalks in spring.

planting.note: Root leaf or stem cuttings in spring.

directions: Grow these plump, egg-shaped pastel leaves in very fast-draining soil and handle them gently to preserve the powdery bloom. Water at the base only when fully dry, easing off in winter. Red-and-orange bells nod from arching stalks in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Root leaf or stem cuttings in spring.

directions: Plant this compact rosette of faceted, jewel-like leaves in gritty mix under bright light to sharpen the pale ridges. Water only when the soil is dry and keep the crown clear of moisture. Red-orange bells rise on short stalks in spring.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring and transplant.

directions: Set this silvery-white, chalky-leaved succulent into coarse, quick-draining soil in full sun. Water sparingly and avoid rubbing off the protective powder. Red flowers rise on tall arching, bracted stems in winter and spring.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring and pot on.

directions: Grow this upright species with its cylindrical, pointed blue-green leaves in gritty mix and full sun. Let it dry fully between waterings and keep water off the leaves. Reddish flowers appear on arching stalks in summer.

planting.note: Root leaf or stem cuttings in spring.

directions: Give this large, robust intergeneric hybrid gritty soil and full sun, where its leaves shift between bronze, purple, and pinkish-yellow with the seasons. Water only when the soil is dry and keep the broad crown clear of moisture. Tall stalks of yellow-and-red star flowers rise in spring.

planting.note: Root leaf cuttings in spring and pot on.

directions: Plant this powdery pinkish-purple hybrid in fast-draining mix and grow in bright light to keep its rich color. Water only when dry and avoid wetting the frosted leaves. Orange-yellow bells appear on arching stalks in spring.

planting.note: Root leaf or stem cuttings in spring.

directions: Set this thick-leaved bluish-pink hybrid into coarse, well-drained soil under strong light for a peachy blush on the leaf tips. Water only when the soil dries and keep the crown dry. Yellow star flowers rise on stalks in spring.

planting.note: Replant offsets in spring.

directions: Grow this clustering, pale cream-and-pink hybrid in gritty mix with bright light to bring out the rosy edges. Water only when fully dry and keep water off the rosettes. Yellow flowers appear on short stalks in spring.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring and transplant once rooted.

directions: Plant this star-shaped rosette, tipped with long red-brown filaments, into fast-draining soil under bright light. Water sparingly and keep the pointed crown dry. Greenish-yellow flowers appear on short stalks in spring.

planting.note: Root leaf cuttings in spring and pot on.

directions: Set this powder-blue rosette into gritty, well-drained soil and grow in bright light to keep the chalky finish. Water only when the mix is dry and keep moisture off the leaves. Pink-and-orange bells rise on arching stems in summer.

planting.note: Push callused cuttings or fallen leaves into gritty mix in spring.

directions: Set individual leaves or short stem tips on fast-draining soil and mist lightly until roots form. Give it strong light so the bean-shaped leaves flush red at the tips rather than staying plain green. Water only when the soil is fully dry, and watch for small yellow star flowers in spring.

planting.note: Press rooted mats or divisions into lean soil in spring.

directions: Firm rooted pieces into gritty soil where they can knit into a low carpet. The scalloped leaves turn deep burgundy in full sun and bright heat, fading toward green in shade. Star-shaped rosy-red flowers cover the mat in summer; shear lightly afterward to keep it tidy.

planting.note: Root callused cuttings in warm, gritty mix in spring.

directions: Let cuttings callus a day or two, then set them in fast-draining soil and water lightly once rooted. Full sun brings out the warm copper-orange leaf tips, while shade keeps the foliage lime green. Fragrant white flowers open in late winter to spring; protect the plant from frost.

planting.note: Insert callused cuttings into warm gritty mix in spring.

directions: Root stem tips in a dry gritty mix and give them bright light to develop the golden-yellow color with rosy edges. Water only when the soil dries out completely, as the fleshy leaves store plenty of moisture. Small white flowers appear in spring, and the plant makes a warm-toned mounding accent.

planting.note: Surface-sow the fine seed or press in fragments in spring.

directions: Sprinkle the tiny seed on gritty soil and keep it barely moist until sprouts appear. The powdery blue-green beads form a dense low cushion that softens rock gardens and trough edges. Pale pink-white flowers rise on short stems in early summer, and the mat tolerates heat and drought well.

planting.note: Sow seed or set divisions in spring into lean soil.

directions: Start from seed or divisions in well-drained ground and give the plant room to form a low spreading clump. Scalloped green leaves take on orange and red tints in autumn. Golden-yellow star flowers open in summer and mature to reddish seed heads that hold interest into fall.

planting.note: Divide or transplant into full sun in early spring.

directions: Set divisions in lean, well-drained soil and full sun to keep the dusky burgundy stems strong and upright. Avoid rich soil and overwatering, which cause the clumps to splay open. Soft pink flower heads on smoky-purple stems open in late summer and dry to attractive seed heads.

planting.note: Root callused stem or leaf cuttings in warm gritty mix.

directions: Set callused cuttings in a fast-draining mix and keep them on the dry side until roots establish. The club-shaped blue-green leaves are tipped red when grown in strong sun. Yellow flowers appear in spring; keep the plant frost-free and avoid summer overwatering.

planting.note: Root cuttings or set divisions in spring in moist gritty soil.

directions: Tuck cuttings into well-drained soil in a spot with some afternoon shade, which keeps the golden leaves from scorching. The rounded chartreuse foliage forms a bright low mat that trails over container edges. Small yellow flowers appear in summer; this one appreciates a touch more moisture than most sedums.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in warm, gritty mix in spring or summer.

directions: Root a callused cutting in dry gritty soil and give it strong light to develop the reddish suction-cup tips on its tubular leaves. Water only when the soil is fully dry, since the fleshy stems rot easily if kept wet. This slow grower makes a sculptural houseplant and may bloom white to pink in winter on established specimens.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix during warm months.

directions: Set a callused cutting in fast-draining soil and keep it bright to bring out red edges on the curled, spoon-like leaves. Let the mix dry fully between waterings to protect the trunk from rot. Like other jades it grows slowly into a small shrub and can produce starry white flowers in winter when mature.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in the warm season.

directions: Grow this cutting-raised jade in the brightest light you can offer so the leaves flush gold, orange, and red at the margins. Water only when the soil dries out completely, and reduce watering in winter. Cool nights and strong sun intensify the sunset coloring; mature plants may bloom white in winter.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in spring or summer.

directions: Lay a callused stem cutting on gritty soil so the paired leaves that spiral up the stem can root. Bright light brings pink edges to the stacked triangular leaves. Small yellowish flowers appear in spring, and the trailing stems look best cascading from a raised pot.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in warm, gritty mix in the growing season.

directions: Insert callused cuttings into a fast-draining mix and provide bright light to bring red edges to the bead-like stacked leaves. Water only when the soil is completely dry. Small clusters of pale flowers open in spring, and the plant makes a charming small container specimen.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix during the warm season.

directions: Root short cuttings in a well-drained mix; the tightly overlapping scale-like leaves give the stems a braided, chain-like look. Give it bright indirect light and let the soil dry between waterings. Tiny greenish-yellow flowers tuck into the leaf axils in spring and are easy to overlook.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in the warm season.

directions: Set a callused cutting in fast-draining soil and grow it in full sun to keep the rounded silvery-blue leaves compact with red margins. Water sparingly and never leave the roots standing in moisture. This slow shrub can reach several feet with age and may open star-shaped white to pink flowers in winter.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in spring or summer.

directions: Root cuttings in a dry, fast-draining mix and expose the plant to strong sun to turn the propeller-like leaves from lime green to fiery red. Cool weather and bright light deepen the color the most. Small fragrant white flowers appear in summer, and the spreading clumps make a bold groundcover accent.

planting.note: Root leaf or stem cuttings in gritty mix in the warm season.

directions: Set callused cuttings in a fast-draining mix and grow them in bright light for sturdy, gray-green sickle-shaped leaves stacked like propeller blades. Water only when the soil is fully dry. Established plants send up showy clusters of scarlet-red flowers in summer that attract pollinators.

planting.note: Root trailing stem cuttings in gritty mix in the warm season.

directions: Lay trailing cuttings on well-drained soil where the heart-shaped leaves can root along the stem. Bright indirect light brings cream, green, and rosy-pink variegation, and cool stress deepens the pink. Let the soil dry between waterings and grow it in a hanging pot so the delicate stems spill over.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in spring or summer.

directions: Root a callused cutting in fast-draining soil and grow it upright in full sun to keep the needle-like leaves densely packed along the stems. Water sparingly and let the mix dry completely between drinks. Small white flowers appear in summer, and the plant is often used to mimic a tree in miniature succulent scenes.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or lay rooted stems in shade in the warm season.

directions: This shade-tolerant crassula roots easily from stems laid on well-drained soil and makes a useful groundcover under trees. The rounded green leaves take on bronze tones in brighter light. Airy sprays of small pink-white star flowers rise above the foliage in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in the warm season.

directions: Grow this collector's hybrid in a gritty mix with bright indirect light, protecting it from harsh midday sun that can scorch the tightly stacked gray-green leaves. Water carefully at the base to avoid trapping water in the folded columns. Reddish flowers may crown the columns in the cooler months.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or set out transplants after frost.

directions: Grow this popular gift succulent in bright indirect light and let the soil dry between waterings to prevent rot. To rebloom, give it about six weeks of long, uninterrupted nights in fall, since the flowers are triggered by short days. Dense clusters of red, orange, pink, yellow, or white flowers last for many weeks.

planting.note: Root leaf or stem cuttings in gritty mix in the warm season.

directions: Root leaf or stem cuttings in a fast-draining mix and grow in bright indirect light to keep the fuzzy silver leaves compact. The felted foliage is edged with chocolate-brown teeth that give the plant its name. Water at the base only when the soil is dry; it rarely blooms indoors but is prized for its velvety leaves.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in the growing season.

directions: Root stem cuttings in well-drained soil and grow in bright light to bring pink and lavender tones to the scallop-edged blue-gray leaves. It spreads readily as stems arch down and root where they touch soil. Bell-shaped orange-brown flowers hang from tall stems in late winter to spring.

planting.note: Root stem or leaf cuttings in gritty mix in the warm season.

directions: Root a stem cutting in a fast-draining mix and give it full sun and warmth to develop the large triangular leaves covered in bronze felt. Water sparingly and never let the roots sit wet. In frost-free gardens it grows into a small tree-like shrub prized far more for its dramatic foliage than its small flowers.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in the growing season.

directions: Root cuttings in a well-drained mix and grow in bright indirect light in a hanging pot so the arching stems can trail. Give it long fall nights to encourage flowering, as with other kalanchoes. Pendant bell-shaped orange-red flowers dangle from the stem tips in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix in the warm season.

directions: Set a callused cutting in a fast-draining mix and grow it in full sun to bring out the cinnamon-copper felt on the young spoon-shaped leaves. The undersides stay silvery, and older leaves fade to gray. Water sparingly; this slow grower forms a small shrub and eventually opens yellow flowers on established plants.
